sentences.text: No, I was not in Mauthausen.
I was in Gross Rosen.
So they started-- I didn't realize that I had a number until after the war.
Because as I told you, they started to give us, now, as I ought to show you, yes?
Can you see something?
This happened in-- oh, it was 1944.
I couldn't tell you exactly.
But I would hardly know what were the days.
KL.
Konzentrationslager.
There's numbers.
Because I I believe they didn't have the numbers.
Till this time, we had been in labor camp.
And labor camp has, according to German law, different law to the-- to Konzentrationslager.
For example, in a concentration camp, when you have to do something extraordinary, or something, there are some rules you have to follow.
In labor camp, the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] head of the SS can do whatever he like.
He was-- there was no rule.
He-- he make the rule, he could change the rule.
I couldn't tell you.
About, three [INAUDIBLE] seven days, a very short time.
Oh, very very funny things.
Can you [INAUDIBLE] 100 people or more male in one room, to sleep?
You know what they are doing with us?
Put it, one over the other.
When you go want to go out to the toilet, you have to jump through the people.
Yes.
About 4 o'clock in the morning, there was was, I believe, some soup.
I don't know, [INAUDIBLE] could be breakfast or dinner, I don't know. 4 o'clock.
And they--
No.
We were being-- sitting and waiting.
But we were sent to Brunnlitz.
No.
Not at all.
Not at all.
According to Mr.
Schindler, we would only be a few days.
And I believe he was right.
We had been-- [? from ?] very short time.
But I [INAUDIBLE] tell you if it was one week or two weeks, I just couldn't, but I believe that it was very short time.
Rosa [? will ?] in Auschwitz.
We have not any-- any knowledge what was going to-- to happen.
She was going as well, I will have to tell you the story.
Very short.
When we have, as I told you that last-- very, [INAUDIBLE] time, we have been working together, and so on.
The workers-- there was no production, so we have-- social-- social with them.

And I was working with my wife, who had been very distressed, you know, because-- I have a belief that when somebody is to Auschwitz to go, or-- I have a belief that we are-- that we are sent to Gross Rosen, that we are going for a-- there's only luck, but then nothing sure with with it.
So we had been distressed, and Schindler was passing.
And, he approached us, and he asked, when you have some valuable, you have to-- I would take it and I will give it back to in Gross Rosen.
I believe he he he goes by the idea that we are well off, because I was very close to Mr.
Bankier.
On first-name, and so on.
Mr.
Bankier has a very well-off people.
So I was supposed to be one of them.
How did he believe that we haven't got needs?
[INAUDIBLE] Whatever I said was, bread, we were dying, the bread, and I put it in a boxes, where the ammunition was supposed to be, two or three boxes.
Before my wife arrived, I ate everything.
[LAUGHS] You ate--
I-- I ate everything what what I sent.
I I asked the Polish people who I was working with, you have to watch me.
These boxes, I make sign on it, nobody [INAUDIBLE] them.
They had been done, they were like, well, they they give you the the boxes.
Yes.
No.
They know what it is.
Dry bread.
Sliced.
Sliced, dry bread.
No.
Ah.
Well, I'm coming back to to to Schindler about that one.
He told us something of this, I don't know, maybe I will not exactly say what he said.
But the meaning was the same.
You have to believe me, when Oskar appeared, it's everything fixed.
You are going to Gross Rosen, you go.
Oh, I was going to-- actually, we'll join you in Brunnlitz.
That was his way.
Whatever, it was exactly how he expressed himself.
Yes.
We-- Well, we tried to believe him.
But we were very distressed.
How we can think that this will happen so.
It was too good to believe.
There was only the one camp, it was.
But you have to ask me, how is it possible for 300 people, or with 1,200 Because we have the list, what Keneally write about, with 1,200 people.
Or 1,100, it doesn't matter.
As I told you, it was a very big cause.
We had been-- our [NON-ENGLISH] of the 800 or so people had been in August, '44.
Because there was such a cause, I believe that they didn't let know Berlin, or the people who had been sending to Mauthausen.
When we had been going to Brunnlitz from Plaszow, all of a sudden, I believe the Jews who will be working in administration find out that we can save another 800 people.
It was probably a very dirty way of working over there, It was money, to be inside, and so on.
But probably, I-- I would like to mention Mr.
Pemper, Mietek Pemper, who was a secretary of Goeth.

One of Jewish secretaries. but he was so [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] clever that when Goeth said something what had to be he had to he couldn't know about it, he was asking one of the German writers to write the letters.
Mr.
Pemper was proving a new [NON-ENGLISH], you have to help me, how does it, called--
Unfortunately, is very little mention about him.
He was a a witness for Goeth, to get in the Krakow court.
That's where, I believe, [BACKROUND NOISE] that happened, all of a sudden, from 300, we have 1,100 or 1,200.
Yeah.
Because we had the the the the the stuff, the same stuff that we had been before the [NON-ENGLISH].
Not the same people.
But I'll tell you, there was-- there was a very dirty word.
[INAUDIBLE]
He was-- I couldn't I couldn't tell you why he had taken such a risk.
I don't know how to explain it now.
I don't know what his mind was in.
Because-- how long we had been working at Emalia he was making money with us.
When he'd been in Brunnlitz, there was no production at all.
Coming back back to that that one was-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] He was getting from the German Wehrmacht some money, how long-- how he progressed, to prepare the factory to work.
When I tell you, those were in-- '40- '45, about there.
He approached me, in you know from the seeing what I had-- done, or said something.
He told me, Presser, I have to-- tomorrow I have an inspection, or after tomorrow, and I have to build the one machine.
Because when they are coming, I get 30,000 or 40,000 marks.
And you know that-- every day costs me 3,000 marks for the Jewish people.
And we haven't got income.
So I understood from him that he lost all the money that he had made in Emalia in supporting the Jewish camp.
Maybe this is-- I am wrong, I am not sure.
But that, according to to the what he told me, I don't understand.
Because 3,000 marks, for four months, is a lot of money.
About six weeks later, five, four weeks later.
I couldn't tell you you know because that we had been I had been straightaway very busy.
To find my boxes and eat what was in the boxes.
The bread from the boxes.
And prepare they all had to be-- remove the textile machines.
Like the Czechoslovakians, who had been doing that so perfectly that each worker was taking one machine to his house.
And they had been having a certain, such a way, a carrying way, that nothing was damaged.
I couldn't tell you how, exactly, because they had been doing wooden cases.
And so, you know the textile machines are very precise.
And they have to do-- otherwise they would be damaged.
They didn't damage anything.
And they didn't-- they had been really hurried.
Yes.
[NON-ENGLISH]
Yes.
[LAUGHS]
She was very nice.
She had a brown coat, and she had a hat cut over her head.
But she looked very nice.
She was in good health.
She had a bread, and she told me, what do I have to do with the bread?
I will keep it.
[LAUGHS]
I can only tell you what Rosa told me.
The German who was in [NON-ENGLISH] gave her bread.
Because he liked her.
My wife, whatever, what else can I explain so?
In Brunnlitz?
No, back in Gross Rosen.
Gross Rosen.
[SIGHS] It's a funny thing.
The chief of the barracks was a German politician who was very polite to us.
But his Polish deputies were shocking.
No.
He-- as I told you, he was a politician.
Because there you can-- they have signs.
Red or-- [INAUDIBLE] [POINTING] you know, we knew by this time, who was here.
At least, somebody explained to us what kind of--
No.
We had the, only the [NON-ENGLISH] paper.
[NON-ENGLISH]
In Gross Rosen, we haven't got a contact with them.
They kept us seperately-- we hadn't got any contact with them.
I had one contact with them.
When I was going to the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to the to to wash myself to to to with the shower.
One Polish man was working there, in the room with the-- steam room.
Whatever that make the steam.
And he told me in Polish, give me everything, because they will take it away from you.
So I had two kits of tobacco, Polish tobacco, shocking.
It can kill you.
There was a knife, and-- [LAUGHS] Anyhow, I see, I have nothing to to say to risk.
So I give it to him.
He told me that-- made an agreement-- come, tomorrow morning, and I'll give you some bonus to go to the-- come in to buy something.
So early in the morning, about 6 o'clock.
After the-- it was a still a dark.
He told me he had to work his shift.
And I go with, he would give me the two bonuses of what I showed you.
I don't know if it was anything.
And I went back.
On my way back, I met the [NON-ENGLISH] the the the politician who was in charge of the barrack.
I don't know.
And he told me, you are stupid.
What you are doing?
They can kill you.
If they-- nobody have to be on the outside.
So he escort me back to the barrack.
From-- about half of September, 1944, to to the 8th of May, 1945.
There was no production, I told you.
It takes a long time to establish and put all the machinery in in a place.
Long time.
Because, not the hard work, but the way we had been doing this from-- there were no tools, you know.
There were no tools.
You have to put a machine who has 36,000 kilos with nothing.
So our work was just like a slave in Egypt doing the pyramids.
Not because we hadn't been hard-working.
Because we hadn't got the tools.
When you have the lifts, who are now around, you come the lift, put it down, and it would take half an hour, one hour, two hours.
But in our case, it could take a week.
To pull one machine inside.
We were rolling and pushing the roll.
It had to be straightened, because it was going side to side, and so on.
Yes, but it was the first time in my life that I know what was hunger.
I have a story about that one.
When Schindler asked me to put the machine in [INAUDIBLE] or something, because he would get there, he told me he would give me a bread.
I'd ask him-- I bargained with him.
I am not doing this for myself, at this time, all the people.
I give him a [INAUDIBLE] as well.
So the next day, when I was-- I having dinner, we had been sitting, the whole family, my mother, my wife, my sister, and the brothers.
And I bring the bread.
I was sure that everybody was OK.
I started to cut the bread, and I feed-- finished the two two kilos of bread but I was still hungry.
That was in my mind, more than in my stomach.
We knew about it a while.
Still, it was the Polish people who were coming from Krakow with these-- some messages, or something of this kind.
Only a few of them had been.
Because we had been late, they took part of the machinery, the very light one, out of the platforms and moved to the factory.
They couldn't move farther because there was still the textile machine.
One was missing, they had to put it-- and also, it was quite a long time.
Very little, exactly.
In Krakow, we have even more than in Brunnlitz.
Because Schindler has a a very expensive cars.
He has a BMW.
He has an Alfa Romeo.
He has a Hudson, American model, with electric wheels.
And he has a German-- very expensive car.
It looked like a Rolls Royce, but he has a powerful radio.
And they have been listening to the radio, or that, we have-- as much as-- as everybody else.
I couldn't tell you about the mood.
But we had been losing 2 2 kilos a week.
They're want to find out who was losing-- who was stealing some bread and potato. 2 kilos a week, we were losing.
We were running to the toilet all the night.
Ooh, [NON-ENGLISH] Passing water.
[INAUDIBLE] Remember.
I told you about the winter they have.
It was-- it was not a good-- [INAUDIBLE]. heating.
So I asked the doctor what I mentioned, the surgeon, he told me, well, Mr.
Schindler had a vitamin C.
We tried to-- so I approached him, and he says, you know by now that he has it.
He goes, back in Poland, the way the cause was, it was [? untrustworthy ?] Jewish men.
I couldn't remember him.
I didn't.
His daughter was-- it was [INAUDIBLE].
And there was there one worker who had to sign, the documents that the Jewish people received it.
And there was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some-- everything was conserved a very good, small-- from-- [NON-ENGLISH]
[NON-ENGLISH] not like a prescription, medication.
And the German take the good one, and probably leave in the supply room, even the bad one.
But this time, he was sure that the war was right around the corner because Russia had been sending in front of the-- on the other side of Vistula.
And he decided to run away.
To bring everything to Schindler.
And Schindler did give us some of the small sometimes.
But in Czechoslovakia, I approached him and asked if he has a vitamin C.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he told me, yes, I can give it to you, but you have to keep it in your hand.
And they have to open it right in front of you and inject you.
Otherwise, they would say, you could put in water.
They give me [INAUDIBLE] of the injection.
And do you believe, that in one week, it started healing, too.
The wound was completely cured.
Pardon?
Alright
Oh, way too much.
No.
It's not too much.
It's very good.
